Good morning everybody. Good afternoon. Welcome. Welcome. Good to see you Ian. Good to see you Leopold. Rash Albert. Johannes. Nice to see you. Venkateram. Always good to see you. We are with a small group today. I see for the first collaboration session. We do it two times a day. We see a lot of people joining when it's US time. But it's good. We work with small groups. So it doesn't matter if we are with a thousand people here or with only a few. So this will work out perfectly. Thanks for joining. And I like to start this session with a bit of teaching around setting up your influence challenge. I think this is one of the most important components if you want to overcome an influence challenge. So let me start by sharing my screen. And I see some people. Extra people are joining. Easto Mark. Welcome. Good to see you. So what are we? What did we email to you as a preparation? So today we're going to work on defining a good influence challenge. Krishna. Good to see you as well. My ever-smiling friend. Yes. So we sent out an email so you could prepare an influence challenge. And this process is actually defined by Dr. Chaldini and Dr. Nidard. You all know Dr. Nidard from his core motors model. And why they define this set of questions? Because often we just have an idea, an influence challenge, and we want to dive right into it. But there's a specific process we teach. And today we start with the application of this process where you will help each other. And the first step is to define an influence challenge that works. An influence challenge you really can work on and make success. And the first starting point we advise always answer these five questions. They seem simple questions, but they are so important to do this properly because this is the starting point. So you actually know what are you going and what do you want to influence. So describe what is the situation? Who is involved? Why is this question? Why is it important to know the situation? Because if you work with a team, what you're going to do today, people need to understand the full picture. They don't need to hear the entire life story, what happened for 20 years. They need a short overview, what is going on. Hello, some people are still joining. Great to see you. So that's the first, who is involved? And this is a funny question because I asked this question many, many times when I did in company consulting. If you ask this to a team of 10 people, you get eight different answers. Who is involved? But that's of course very important because to define who is involved, those are the people we need to influence. Then the third question, what do you hope to accomplish? Why is this such an important question? Because this gives a clear objective. By answering this question, you know what you want to achieve, especially in a role if you do this as a consultant. We, in the beginning days, I know these stories from Dr. Cialdini, they didn't ask this question. So what happened? They had a great result, let's say the desired result increased with 20%. And then they got as a reply. Not thank you, that's great. The reply was always, why isn't it 40%. So by defining when do you have success, you can celebrate the success and you already, and you know that is a success. So that's why the third question is so important. What barriers exist to obtaining a positive outcome? Again, in teamwork, this is a very important question because most of the time people see where the restriction. People already have ideas. If we can only overcome this hurdle, probably the situation will go a lot better or a lot smoother. So this will give you a lot helpful information. And the last, what are some solutions to the problem you have been considering or trying? Because considering means you not tried it, and trying means, of course, to implement it, but it didn't work. These questions are crucial to sketch a more complete picture when working together solving influence challenges. So these questions we also send out as a preparation. I understand you're all busy, so you might didn't have time to do it. What I recommend at this moment, because you're going to work on influence cases in every collaboration session, if you didn't do that, please take a print screen or a picture from these five questions so you can have them in front of you when in the rest of this session you will start working together. So I give you a second to take a print screen, a picture, and best may ask a question, please. For sure. Yes. When I went through the questions, I saw a small difference, because for some leaders, the positive business outcome might be important, but there might be an emotive business outcome that is different than the business outcome, just with some leaders who might want to have a solution. But in the end for them, it's maybe just to get some stars on the chest to shine off in front of the, I don't know, the boss, which is completely different. So does it make sense to split this into business outcomes and emotive outcomes? That could be great. And motive outcome is of course mostly a personal driver. I like the best is that the business outcome is leading. I will dive into this. So this session, most collaboration sessions, I will direct a way of thinking. This specific session, I have some teaching prepared to guide that way of thinking. So I will come in a more detailed sense back to your question in a few moments. But what I can say, make the business outcome leading, because if the business outcome is successful, the personal motive of getting a tip on the shoulder will follow. Good. But the business outcome could also be, I need to get that tip of on my shoulder to make a promotion. So then it's in the influence challenge, but then again, the business outcome is leading because the promotion is the goal. Okay, clear. Yep, perfect. So I think everybody now had time to make a print screen or write these questions. These questions are by the way also in our practitioner program in the action plan. But now you get a more broad sense. Why is this so important? And in a second, you will get even a broader sense. Why is this so important? What we always say, these principles sound misleadingly easy. Then by applying it, it becomes harder application is hard to do that on the highest possible level. But actually, the most difficult thing about influence is knowing exactly what you want to influence. So often the starting point where people, where people start is often wrong. So you know, if you start from a wrong perspective, you cannot reach the outcome which you could reach. Probably you can, you can be successful, but you could probably be more successful. And here we start by learning how to do that. The question you primarily should ask yourself is, what do I want my target of influence to do? And target of influence can be a website visitor, can be your colleagues, can be your clients, can be your child, can be your spouse, your friend, because we are trying, we communicate all the time to get things done from other people. So the broad term is the target of influence for some people that might literally translate it sounds negative, but that's definitely not the intention here. It's just who are we focusing on to target to influence in an ethical way? And why, what do we need to do? We need to define the behavior. You cannot influence an outcome. You can influence the behavior that leads to an outcome. And I always like to explain it this way. So often companies, people set a goal. And those goals are, I want more leads, I want more sales. Or hot topic now today, I want an inclusive work environment. Going on in many companies, goals like this, a safer psychological safe work environment, you see these topics coming all the time. But if you said that there's a goal as a company or as a person, you right away say something else. You also say, at this moment, we don't have an inclusive work environment. Otherwise, why would you set the goal to create it if you already have it, right? So if people set the goal, then simultaneously say you don't have it right now. And now, now people or they hire big experts, they spend a lot of money and they say, we need to go from A to B. And then you get all these fantastic ideas. But often this process fails. Let me explain you why. How an influence challenge works is, if you now say in this example, I don't have an inclusive work environment, you need to define what behaviors do people show. The behavior could be, there could be all kinds of behaviors, why a work environment is not inclusive. A simple one HR only hires males, white males within the age of 30 to 50. Well, that could be a behavior that HR is doing it. So you need to define the behaviors which leads to the not inclusive work environment. Then what you need to do is to define the behaviors that will lead to the inclusive work environment. And now you can make success because now you can influence behaviors. You cannot influence, okay, let's create an inclusive work environment. These are subtle nuances, but super important. You can influence behavior that leads to an outcome. So to make successful projects, you need to have this line. And often it's only I want to go from A to B. To continue there, and let me go back, oh, sorry for that. Here, these are the questions. You need to know how to define behaviors. I like to give this simple example, some of you may seen me using it. This is a simple landing page. And what does Digital River once there, in this case, visit us to do? It's very simple. They want people to download an e-book. Now we go to download an e-book. That sounds simple. I want to reach that behavior. But that journey contains of micro behaviors. And the micro behaviors in this case probably are people need to click on an ad somewhere. They have to read the promotional text that starts seeing 2020 and beyond. Then they need to fill out the form. Then they have to give some consent with the privacy policy. And then they have to press that in this case, get the e-book or they submit the form. So something simple as downloading an e-book already contains of five micro behaviors. Now you can use the principles of influence to let more people click on your ad. So more people go from step one to step two, from step two to step three, from step and so on. So you boost the end result. If you define your influence challenge like this, you have also much more control over the outcome because every micro behavior could have a different hurdle or a different objective, why people not moving forward. For example, click on the ad, maybe their social proof and authority do the job. But if they need to submit the form, it could be scarcity or consistency that does the job. So per micro behavior, we can connect the core motors model. So what do we see? Which signals do we see? Blocking us from people doing the micro behavior, go to the core motors model of Dr. Nydert, select the right set of principles through the activators and amplifiers, we can start applying it. So what we're going to do today and is define your influence challenge. During our practitioner program and during this mastermind, you will get a lot of tools and a systematic way to overcome these influence challenges. That's why I love this session. This is the beginning. If you do this the best way possible, your outcome will be much more successful because you have a detailed action plan. And how do we going to do this? These are collaboration sessions, so you're going to do this with your peers. And also for working together because you like to use group intelligence. We're all here together with people that are mastering influence, are already masters of influence, do the practitioner program, follow the mastermind sessions to have in depth training and want to practice ongoing on practical application. But to do so Dr. Chalbini also developed a process on how can you collaborate together with other people. And that's called the influence process. And I love this process because it will save you a lot of time if you are able to do this with like-minded people. We all have been in brainstorm sessions and if you are a bit like me, you experienced, well, we all come together in a room. One person says, well, this is my problem. And then everybody in the room dives right into it, creating all kinds of potential solutions. In the end, it becomes a contest who has the loudest voice. And after an hour of shouting and yelling to each other, we walk outside without an action plan. And that was the brainstorm session. That's what happens a lot of times in brainstorm, business environment, organizational environment. That's not very efficient. We already, everybody nowadays is packed with work, packed with information overload. So we need to do it in a much more efficient way. And today is also our first day that we practice this together. The influence process contains four super simple steps. But just like these five questions, crucial steps. The first step is when you start collaborating, you do a step back. So the person that brings in the topic, which needs a solution, he explains what is the situation, who is involved, what do you like to accomplish, what barriers do you see, and what solutions did you already consider. So these are these five questions I explained in the beginning of this session. By doing that, your audience that you like to participate with, that you like to collaborate with, have a much better view of what you're trying to solve, than just say, oh, let's create inclusive work environment. Because what happens if people start generating ideas, it goes sideways. Because they don't really, really understand, they understand for 80, 85% what you're trying to achieve, but they need to understand it 100%. So there's idea generation based on that challenge. If it's only 85%, the room goes sideways. So step back. This only should take five minutes. So when you go into the breakout session in a second, the first thing you do is assign a timekeeper. We do this with small groups of three or four, depends if the group is the possible be divided by three, but you are with three or four people in the breakout session. First step is assign a timekeeper to keep the meeting efficient. Timekeeper times five minutes for the person that starts. In five minutes, that person will explain his or her case. And now something funny happens. Normally, when you do this in brainstorm sessions, let's say it happens, that people really did a good briefing before people start brainstorming. Then still everybody starts talking. Well, that's a slight mistake because what actually should you do when people ask you to solve influence challenge? The best idea probably is to think, use your brains before your talk. And this is actually quite funny because that never happens in brainstorm session. And if you're trying to implement this with people, with other people for the first time, it feels weird. Because if you think on the sound volume in the room, it means step back. One person is talking. Step two, thinking is an individual process. So that means there's no sound. But can you imagine that you go into a brainstorm session, one person says something, and then it's silent. That feels really weird. We are not used to it, but it's much more efficient. Actually, research shows that if you think for seven minutes, that you come to about 88, can come to about 80, 85% of your best ideas on that case. If you have a proper way or a systematic way of thinking, in our case, we guide that thinking process. Because if we think about an influence challenge, we heard the challenge, we heard the signals, then we think through the Cormotus model to a set of principle and through these principles to a set of ideas based on activators and amplifiers. Today, we're going to do this slightly different. Today, we hear, what is your influence challenge? And we're going to think, what are these micro behaviors that will lead to that, to solve that outcome? So we're going to help each other form a proper influence challenge. So we use that challenge to move forward. And this exercise, I recommend to do that with every client. This is already very valuable. If you have clients and you're helping them, this is valuable to do with your colleagues. Again, because your starting point is so much better, so your outcomes will be much more successful. And then after we had some thinking time, still we feel, oh, I need to talk and we need to start discussing all the ideas. No, what we should do is listen to every idea. So we ask solicit input. And that doesn't mean that every person gets all the time in the world and can stare their life story and that they also were in this situation. That's very interesting, but we don't want to hear that every person get, if you are with four people, you get two minutes per person. If you are with three people, you get two and a half minutes per person, and you tell your best ideas. So it's very focused. The sound level at that moment in the room is again, one person talking. Why is this important? Because sometimes the cleaning person can have a better idea as the CEO of a big organization, depending on what the case is about. It's about how clean is the environment. That person works on it every day, not the CEO. But if the CEO says something, we intend to agree. So you want to hear every idea and you want to hear it in a certain order. Because what I just said, if there's a hierarchy in a company and every company eventually has a hierarchy, you want the person that say the lowest in rank needs to start. Research also shows if a CEO or a manager higher up the chain, leads a meeting and they say, I think this is the best idea, the rest of the room intends to agree. They stop being creative themselves. They stop sharing their ideas, which could be much better. So if there's hierarchy, that's in your case today. That's not the case, but if you use this exercise, be aware if there's hierarchy, make sure the person lowest in rank goes first. And then you create an action plan. What is an action plan? Also in business, we often write full reports. So there's a note taker and they start working for a day and you get like a 30, 40 page document with all kinds of notes from a one or two hour meeting. Two things will happen with that document. One, everybody it sent to, nobody reads it. So for that point of view, it's a waste of time. Second, the person who is writing those documents get angered. They're going to perceive that as the sole truth. Well, in influence, there is no just one truth because the science of persuasion is not a black and white science. It's not the case. If we say this, everybody in the world will always do that. So you need to stay flexible. So what do we want to create as an action plan? A list of bullet points, a list of ideas based on thought process and those ideas we're going to implement and see the outcome. So that's a simple one page document with bullet points of ideas. What are we going to do? Today, we're going to work on our cases in the first collaboration session like I just explained. So what I like you to do, if you go into the breakout room together, first thing, and before we do that, I see so I feel as hands up, I will answer the question. First thing you do, you assign a timekeeper. The timekeeper does the following things. Every person in the room gets five minutes. No, sorry. Second thing you do, first thing, timekeeper. Second thing you do, you define the order in which you're going to handle the cases. You will be three or four people. They define, we talk, let's say, Pilar, Albert, and Leo Paul are in a room. They define Pilar, go first, Albert, second, Leo Paul goes third, and you help with each other's cases. Then the first person starts. The first person gets five minutes to explain their influence challenge based on those five questions that I just answered. If you don't need five minutes, you just continue. You don't have to wait. It does nothing more to say. You don't ask deep dive questions about the case. It's the goal that the person who brings in the case has to be clear. So they define the case. You move to the next step. The next step is thinking time. We do seven minutes of thinking. If you are with four, we use it to five, please, because then we will be finished at the same time. So sound level is silent. What are we thinking about? We think about a set of micro behaviors, but not more than 10 micro behaviors. Otherwise, you have a too complex case. I saw that with online influence that people start from an ad to a full website and web shop journey and then do the checkout. That's maybe a hundred micro behaviors. You break them up. Otherwise, you lose track of what you are trying to solve. So this also leads to defined specific influence challenges. That's thinking time. So everybody starts thinking, what are these micro behaviors? So let's say input. If you are with three people, a person gets maxed two and a half minutes. So with four, two minutes. And they mention, okay, I think this is the sequence of micro behaviors for your influence challenge. You don't reply. You don't say, yeah, but I think, oh, yeah, that's a good idea. You wait. You can make notes, but you don't interrupt the person who is speaking at the solicit input phase. And then you get 10 minutes to create an action plan. And in this case, the action plan is come up with the best set of micro behaviors that will lead to the outcome that the other person wants to achieve. So per case, five minutes, then depending on if you are with four or three people in a group, three people, seven minutes, thinking time, four people, five solicit input, everybody gets a turn. Four people in a group, you have max two minutes, three people in a group, max two and a half minutes. Then you create an action plan. Four people in a group, eight minutes, three people, 10 minutes. But that will wrap up the entire process in one and a half hours, and you can do three to four cases and you collaborate. And this is interesting because I promise you one thing, you have your influence challenge in your industry, but you get some kind of blinders if you work all day in a certain industry. Blinders are like what horses have. So you get stared in one direction. So having input from other people, that will also be, you also mentioned, see this happening on influence challenges later in collaboration sessions, when we are going to apply these principles together, you'll get great ideas because they don't have these blinders. They don't have these objections that you have because you work every day on these cases. I see two questions. And if anybody else has another question, feel free to raise your hand. Thank you, Bas. I think what the, what we will discover during these first sessions of the master mind that people find those things the most difficult defining the influence challenge, be very specific what the goal is, make micro behaviors, then we dive into contrast. That's always a thing that people find difficult. And then idea generation on the principles, our brain works in a way that we think that's more simple. So, but that's also the reason why many people don't succeed at the level where they could succeed because they don't, they struggle in the beginning. So to answer your question properly, how do I do this? It takes preparation. So like we also teach this sleut of influence, does their homework, and what is the situation you now learned? Okay, I have to be very specific on what is going on. And if you address that specific, so I want specific making up some story. I have a web shop and my clients do watch my products, they put them in the shopping cart, but then they don't buy. That's very a specific influence challenge. If you have dressed that in a group, people right away are focused, okay, I get that. So I think there is the magic, trying to realize and define as specific as possible. What is going on? What do you want? Can you ask questions when somebody is defining their challenge? Can you try to guide them to that final thing? Because that's the thing I struggle the most. I get that. Not great. Thanks, too. Thank you so much. Please, too. Check your messages quickly. I'd send your message. Yeah. I don't know. Mark, I think you were first. Now it's slightly changed in my screen. But Mark. Okay. What I noticed here, which I've also noticed in real practice, if you like, is that people, we struggle to get to the micro behaviors. And this was mentioned before. People tend to jump immediately on. You constantly have to pull people back and say, well, hold on. First of all, you need to get them to actually answer the phone. Forget about what you're going to tell them. Let's talk about how you get them to actually speak with you. Is that okay? That is the right approach, right? Constantly back to, okay, micro, micro. That's the right approach. And one thing that helps, and I gave this example, I think multiple times. So my apologies if you hear this, it's reasons why that's that copy machine study where people in the 60s were waiting in line for a copy machine. You still have to go to a machine, put in some dollars or euros or whatever currency you had at that time in the 60s and make copies. Well, there's a line and then they tested, can I skip the line? No reason. 60% said yes. Then they go, can I skip the line because I have to teach my class and I completely forgot to make these copies? A reason, a valid reason. 94% said yes. Now the fun part comes, then somebody comes in, can I skip the line because I need to make copies? What does the rest in line for a copy machine need to do? I think they need to make copies, right? 90% said yes. So if you give people a reason why this is important, even if the reason is not so valid, people are much more motivated to do what you ask them to do. This is a persuasion technique, which I called in my book, Reasons Why, but it's also a very powerful technique guiding clients or colleagues. For example, explaining what I just did with the non-inclusive or the inclusive work environment, why are we defining behaviors, suddenly you realize this is important to do and this is important to do it step by step because now I see I can go from one to two to three to five. So yes, now I understand people need to pick up the phone first before I can start talking. By framing that picture, by sketching that picture, I think your clients will be more likely to do it in that order because they now see the importance and you give them the reason why. Yeah, and the focus of applying the principles and all this wonderful stuff actually becomes okay, how do I get them to listen to me on the phone? Exactly, and now you have microbehavior one. That's, I don't know, a call center agent and they always are in their script. It's first why people answer your phone call, then the first five seconds why they start listening if you are a call center because most people like to hang up the phone. So those are micro behaviors you then define in that case and principles, different principles work more successful in each micro behavior, but by boosting each result, you will boost the end result. That's why I always also say this is so important what we did today because it will frame a journey and each influence challenge basically contains of micro influence challenges and if you can define those with those micro behaviors, you can boost the outcome enormously. And that could be only a sentence and let's say stick in this example for the call center agent. The first thing that comes out of your mouth makes if you can do your pitch yes or no. So that's your first influence challenge. If somebody willing to listen, first sentence does the job. Same with what Johannes does, email marketing. The subject line is so important to open the email. Then the first couple of sentences in his email, do I read the full email? Those are all micro influence challenges. If you look at it that way, now we can really make an impact for ourselves, for colleagues, for clients. That's helpful. I just wanted to highlight, Pila demonstrated that in her request to go first. Really, Pila? She gave a reason because what I want to say is related to the process. So she is mastering this material. Ah, yes, great job. The sleut of influence. And then also funny in language because it's indeed the word because does the magic. And in Dutch, for example, Albert can understand and mark what I say now. We have basically two words for because we have a word called on that we have a word one and it says the same, but the effect is totally different. After the word on that that's research is perceived as facts. After one is perceived as an opinion. And what is powerful factual driven. So in Dutch, you should say on that. So it's my word, even in your local language to study. If you have also these phrase, I know German, Johannes has definitely different word for it. So that but because I understand a bit German finish, I don't know. But it's interesting to go online maybe and see if you have similar type of research because the word can matter. Absolutely. This last question. Yeah, hey, just one question. I loved everything we did because on the one hand, it showed your openness and willingness to get others views and also respect the voice of what they are saying. And this is true listening. This is respect. And what I believe this is, this works very well. But what about situation where the stress level is very high in the room where you know difficult things are coming up and you see that one or two or three people are not behaving like you would them in such a session. How do you handle such room to still keep this going? By standing there as the instructor and say, okay, please be silent. I was in training sessions full of executives that always had their opinion. They also value their opinion because that was basically why they are in that position. You just correct them. And again, in a nice way, we say we're using this process as science-based. It will teach you, you give reasons why, teach you in a more effective, a more efficient timeframe to get more effective outcomes. I know this is not what you used to, but please try. Okay. So if seven minutes thinking time, it means silence in the room and don't stare one, don't do one minute of thinking and stare out of the window for six minutes. That's also sometimes, please keep on thinking and focus. So just correct them, but again with subtle phrases. Okay, which means you should have the guts to correct the CEO and just say, stay quiet for seven minutes. Something like that. And I will promise you, people respect that a lot. Because they asked you to give them tooling to do it. And they are not used to getting corrected, these executives, but they respect if people do it. Good. Thank you. In this situation, was that helpful? Yeah, it was just helpful just to understand if you can be, like you said, in Dutch way, it's very direct. We are very direct, although, but I had people all over the world. So I know the cultural difference. In the Netherlands, you can say everything you like.
